parts.label: Part 1
parts.instructions: Read the text and choose the best answer.
parts.passage: Linguists point out that so-called loanwords are not inherently a threat to a language; rather, they reflect long-standing contact between cultural and economic spheres. What matters is whether a borrowing names a precise concept or merely offers a fashionable substitute for an established term.

options.text: Every loanword necessarily harms the language.
The value of loanwords depends on their function.
Loanwords arise only from economic pressure.

parts.label: Part 1
parts.instructions: Read the transcript and choose the best answer.
parts.transcript:
Host: Professor Reed, you advocate reforming academic English. What is your main criticism?
Prof. Reed: Many texts become entangled in nominal style and passive constructions. That hinders access for non-specialists and complicates translation because meaning is buried in abstraction.

items.question: What does Professor Reed mainly criticise?

options.text: Too many foreign words in academia
A lack of translated academic texts

parts.task: Write a cohesive essay (about 200 words) on the extent to which multilingualism in international teams creates advantages or risks for productivity.
Discuss the claim: "In a globalised world, national culture is losing significance." Explore the strengths and limits of this thesis.
parts.note: 2 minutes preparation — Evaluated with AI in the full app.
